hypothesis: Incorporating a Dirichlet-distributed noise process into the forward diffusion chain—replacing the standard Gaussian noise schedule with one that inherently respects the simplex geometry of compositional data—will simultaneously eliminate the need for post-hoc regularization penalties and improve both the density and coverage of generated composite compositions compared to PCDiff's penalty-based constraint enforcement, because the forward process will never leave the feasible composition space, making the learned reverse process geometrically consistent with the non-negativity and sum-to-one constraints by construction rather than by approximation.
